subject: Biology
unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
questions.question: Which mechanical soil conservation measure involves constructing step-like structures on steep slopes to reduce runoff velocity?
questions.explanation: Terracing creates level steps on slopes, reducing water flow speed and soil erosion. Contour ploughing (A) follows contours; crop rotation (C) and mulching (D) are biological/agronomic measures.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia

explanations_tiered.definition: Terracing is a mechanical soil conservation method that creates level steps on steep slopes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Terraces shorten slope length and reduce runoff velocity, minimising soil erosion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Terraces are like stairs cut into a hill, so water doesn't rush down and wash soil away.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Terracing converts a steep slope into a series of flat or gently sloping platforms, intercepting overland flow.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Konso people of Ethiopia have practiced dry-stone terracing for over 400 years, recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage site.
reasoning_steps: Identify mechanical measure
Recall terraces as step-like structures
Distinguish from ploughing or mulching
misconceptions: Contour ploughing follows hillside curves but does not create steps; terracing creates steps.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Terracing = steps on slopes, reduces erosion.

glossary_entry.definition: Step-like structures on slopes to reduce soil erosion.

flashcard.front: What is terracing in soil conservation?
flashcard.back: Creating level steps on steep slopes to slow runoff.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Step-like structures on slopes to reduce runoff and erosion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Mechanical soil conservation using level steps on steep land.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Terracing = steps on hills, stops erosion.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show Konso terracing as Ethiopian indigenous practice.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Terracing creates step-like structures. Contour ploughing (A) follows contours without steps.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
questions.question: How does deforestation directly contribute to soil erosion in Ethiopia?

options.A: Trees release chemicals that bind soil particles
options.B: Removing tree cover eliminates root systems that anchor soil and reduces rainfall interception
options.D: Trees prevent all rainfall from reaching the ground

questions.hint: Tree roots hold soil; canopy slows rain impact.
questions.explanation: Tree roots bind soil, and canopy intercepts rain, reducing splash erosion. Deforestation removes both protections. Option B correctly describes this. Options A and C are false; D is an overstatement.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
source_trace.content_anchor: causes of soil erosion are deforestation and poor land husbandry

explanations_tiered.definition: Deforestation increases soil erosion by removing root anchorage and canopy cover.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Tree roots bind soil; canopy reduces raindrop impact. Loss of both accelerates runoff and soil loss.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Trees hold soil with their roots like a net. Without trees, rain washes soil away easily.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Deforestation increases splash erosion (no canopy) and reduces soil aggregate stability (no roots), leading to gully formation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) includes a cover-management factor \(C\); deforestation increases \(C\) dramatically, raising erosion rates.
reasoning_steps: Identify two protective functions: roots anchor soil, canopy intercepts rain.
Removing trees removes both protections.
Conclusion: deforestation increases erosion.
misconceptions: Some think trees only affect erosion through roots, ignoring canopy interception.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Deforestation → no roots (unstable soil) + no canopy (rain splash).

glossary_entry.definition: Removal of forest cover, increasing soil erosion risk.

flashcard.front: Two ways trees prevent soil erosion?
flashcard.back: Roots anchor soil; canopy intercepts rain reducing splash.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does deforestation worsen soil erosion?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Removes root anchorage and canopy interception, increasing runoff and splash erosion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Forest removal leading to soil erosion and habitat loss.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Deforestation = no roots + no canopy = more erosion.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Ethiopian highlands as example: rapid erosion after deforestation.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Roots anchor soil; canopy intercepts rain. Deforestation removes both.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
questions.question: Which of the following is a biological soil conservation measure?
questions.explanation: Agroforestry uses trees and crops together, a biological measure. Check dams (A), diversion ditches (B), and stone bunds (D) are mechanical/structural measures.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
source_trace.content_anchor: biological measures include tree seedling planting and agroforestry

explanations_tiered.definition: Biological soil conservation uses living plants to protect and improve soil.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Agroforestry, cover cropping, and mulching are biological; structures like dams are mechanical.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Agroforestry means growing trees with crops. Trees hold soil with roots — that's living (biological) protection.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Biological measures enhance soil structure and cover; mechanical measures physically redirect or block water flow.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Agroforestry systems increase soil organic carbon, improve infiltration, and reduce runoff by up to 50% compared to bare soil.
reasoning_steps: Recall two categories: mechanical (structures) vs biological (plants).
Agroforestry uses trees → biological.
Check dams, ditches, bunds are structures → mechanical.
misconceptions: Some classify tree planting as 'structural', but it's biological because living roots and canopy provide protection.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Biological = plants (agroforestry, mulching). Mechanical = structures (dams, bunds).

glossary_entry.definition: Land-use system combining trees with crops for soil conservation and productivity.

flashcard.front: Is agroforestry a biological or mechanical conservation measure?
flashcard.back: Biological (uses living trees for soil protection).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give an example of biological soil conservation.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Biological soil conservation using trees and crops together.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Biological = agroforestry, mulching. Mechanical = bunds, ditches.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate mechanical and biological soil conservation
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Agroforestry uses trees (biological). Others are mechanical structures.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.4 Impact of traffic accident on wild and domestic animals
questions.question: How do roads and highways primarily cause declines in wildlife populations?

options.A: By providing new habitats for predators
options.B: By causing direct mortality through vehicle collisions (roadkill)
options.C: By increasing food availability for herbivores

questions.explanation: Roadkill is a direct cause of mortality, especially for species that must cross roads to access resources. Option B identifies this primary impact. Options A, C, and D are not typical effects; roads actually increase noise.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.4 Impact of traffic accident on wild and domestic animals
source_trace.content_anchor: car accidents are the major cause of death for both wild and domestic animals

explanations_tiered.definition: Roadkill is direct mortality from vehicle collisions, a major cause of wildlife population decline.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Animals crossing roads are killed, reducing population size, especially for species with large home ranges.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When a car hits a deer crossing the road, that deer dies. Many such deaths reduce the population.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Road ecology studies show that roadkill can be a significant mortality source, sometimes exceeding natural predation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For some species, roadkill mortality exceeds sustainable harvest limits, causing population declines and local extirpation.
reasoning_steps: Identify that roads cut through habitats.
Recognise animals must cross roads.
Collisions cause direct death.
misconceptions: Some think roads only affect animals via habitat loss; direct mortality (roadkill) is often more immediate.

glossary_entry.definition: Animal killed by vehicle collision on roads.

flashcard.front: How do roads directly reduce wildlife populations?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the main direct impact of roads on wildlife?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Wildlife death due to vehicle collision.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Roads kill animals directly via collisions.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Ethiopian studies: 29% of wildlife mortality in Geralle National Park from car accidents.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain impact of traffic accidents on animals
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Vehicle collisions cause direct mortality. Other options are incorrect or minor.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.4 Impact of traffic accident on wild and domestic animals
questions.question: Which engineering solution is designed to reduce wildlife-vehicle collisions by allowing animals to cross safely under or over a road?
questions.hint: Structures that let animals cross without touching traffic.
questions.explanation: Wildlife underpasses (tunnels) and overpasses (green bridges) provide safe crossing points, reducing roadkill. Higher speeds (A) increase collisions; removing vegetation (C) may worsen habitat fragmentation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.4 Impact of traffic accident on wild and domestic animals
source_trace.content_anchor: designing structures that allow safe passage for animals and wildlife, constructing underpasses

explanations_tiered.definition: Wildlife underpasses and overpasses are structures that allow animals to cross roads safely.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: These structures maintain habitat connectivity and drastically reduce roadkill.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An underpass is like a tunnel under the road so animals don't have to cross the pavement.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Overpasses (green bridges) are vegetated; underpasses may be culverts or box tunnels. Both reduce collisions by 80-95%.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Road ecology recommends crossing structures at intervals of 2-5 km, combined with fencing to funnel animals to safe crossing points.
reasoning_steps: Identify problem: animals cross roads unsafely.
Solution: provide dedicated crossing points (under/over).
These separate animals from traffic.
misconceptions: Some think higher fences alone work, but fences without crossing points trap animals on roads.

glossary_entry.definition: Structure allowing animals to cross under a road safely.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Safe crossing structure below road surface.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Mention that Banff National Park (Canada) overpasses reduced collisions by 80%.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify methods to reduce traffic accident impacts on animals
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Wildlife underpasses/overpasses provide safe crossing, reducing collisions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.question: The energy produced by a hydroelectric dam is renewable because the water cycle is powered by solar energy. Which formula represents the gravitational potential energy converted to electricity in falling water?
questions.hint: Potential energy depends on mass, gravity, height.
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy is \(E = mgh\), where \(m\) = mass, \(g\) = gravity, \(h\) = height. Option C is correct. Option B is kinetic energy; Option A is mass-energy equivalence.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources

explanations_tiered.definition: Gravitational potential energy \(E = mgh\) is converted to kinetic energy in falling water for hydropower.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Water stored behind a dam has high potential energy; releasing it converts \(mgh\) to electricity via turbines.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water at the top of a dam has stored energy because of its height. When it falls, that energy turns turbines.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The potential energy \(mgh\) (mass × gravity × height) is transformed to kinetic energy and then to electrical energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The maximum power available is \(P = \rho Q g h\) (density × flow rate × gravity × head).
reasoning_steps: Recall that falling water has gravitational potential energy.
Formula for gravitational potential energy: \(E = mgh\).
Exclude kinetic energy formula (B) and others.
misconceptions: Some confuse potential (\(mgh\)) with kinetic (\(\frac12 mv^2\)); both are involved in energy conversion, but stored energy in reservoir is potential.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy stored due to height: \(E = mgh\).

flashcard.front: Formula for potential energy of water in a dam?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy stored due to position or height.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to GERD: water stored at height → potential energy → electricity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply basic physics to renewable energy concepts
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(E = mgh\) is gravitational potential energy for falling water.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.question: A country has \(2.5 \times 10^9\) tonnes of coal reserves and consumes \(5.0 \times 10^7\) tonnes per year. If consumption grows at 2% per year, will the reserves last longer or shorter than the static depletion time?

options.B: Shorter, because increasing consumption accelerates depletion
options.C: The same, because growth does not affect total

questions.explanation: Static depletion time = reserves/current consumption = \(2.5e9 / 5e7 = 50\) years. With 2% annual growth, consumption increases each year, depleting reserves faster (shorter time). Option B correctly identifies this.

calculation_steps: With 2% growth, annual consumption increases each year

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources

explanations_tiered.definition: When consumption grows, depletion occurs faster than static calculation because each year uses more than the previous.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Static depletion time assumes constant consumption; exponential growth in consumption shortens the actual depletion time significantly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you eat 2 cookies today, 3 tomorrow, 4 the next day, you'll finish the jar faster than if you ate 2 every day.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: With 2% annual growth, consumption doubles every \(\ln(2)/0.02 \approx 35\) years, accelerating depletion.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hubbert's peak and logistic model of depletion show that with growth, the peak production occurs earlier, and total exhaustion time is less than static \(R/C_0\).
worked_solution: With growth, actual T < 50 years because consumption increases over time.
reasoning_steps: Calculate static time: 50 years.
Recognise that consumption growth increases annual demand.
Conclude actual depletion time is shorter.
misconceptions: Many assume static depletion time is accurate, ignoring consumption growth.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Consumption growth → faster depletion (actual T < static T).

glossary_entry.definition: Years to exhaust reserves assuming constant consumption.

flashcard.front: Does consumption growth make depletion faster or slower?
flashcard.back: Faster; static estimate assumes constant use, but growth increases annual consumption.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does consumption growth affect depletion time?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Growth accelerates depletion, making actual time shorter than static \(R/C\) estimate.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Annual increase in resource use, accelerating depletion.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use contrast: static vs exponential growth model for consumption.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Growth increases annual consumption → faster depletion (shorter than static 50 years).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.question: Under what condition is a renewable resource (such as a forest) effectively non-renewable on human timescales?

options.A: When it is protected in a national park
options.B: When the harvest rate exceeds the regrowth rate for an extended period
options.C: When it is used for ecotourism

questions.hint: Overharvest turns renewable into de facto non-renewable.
questions.explanation: If harvest exceeds regrowth, the resource stock declines; if sustained, it may be depleted below recovery threshold, becoming effectively non-renewable. Option B describes this. Options A, C, D do not cause non-renewability.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.content_anchor: renewable resources can be depleted if overused

explanations_tiered.definition: A renewable resource becomes effectively non-renewable when harvest rate consistently exceeds regrowth rate.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Overexploitation (e.g., deforestation faster than replanting) leads to stock depletion and possible local extinction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you cut trees faster than they can grow back, eventually there are no trees left — it's like a non-renewable mine.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Maximum sustainable yield (MSY) is the harvest rate equal to regrowth. Exceeding MSY reduces the standing stock; prolonged overharvest can collapse the population.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The logistic harvest model shows that if harvest \(H > rN(1-N/K)\), population \(N\) decreases to zero, making the renewable resource functionally non-renewable.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of renewable: can regrow within human lifespan.
If harvest > regrowth, stock declines.
If decline continues, regrowth may become impossible (depleted below minimum viable population).
misconceptions: Renewable does not mean 'infinite if mismanaged'. Overharvest can destroy renewability.

glossary_entry.definition: Maximum harvest rate that does not deplete a renewable resource.

flashcard.front: When does a renewable resource become effectively non-renewable?
flashcard.back: When harvest rate exceeds regrowth rate for an extended period.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can a renewable resource be depleted?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Harvesting a renewable resource faster than it can regrow.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Examples: Atlantic cod fishery collapse, deforestation in Madagascar.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain conditions that lead to resource depletion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Harvest > regrowth reduces stock; if sustained, resource is effectively non-renewable.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.question: Why is natural gas considered a non-renewable resource even though it is a cleaner-burning fuel than coal?

options.A: Because it produces no pollutants when burned
options.B: Because it forms over millions of years from ancient organic matter, and consumption exceeds formation
options.C: Because it is not found in Ethiopia
options.D: Because it is used only for heating

questions.hint: Non-renewability depends on formation time, not cleanliness.
questions.explanation: Natural gas, like other fossil fuels, takes millions of years to form; current consumption vastly exceeds formation rate. Cleanliness (lower CO2 per energy unit) does not change renewability. Option B correctly states this.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.content_anchor: fossil fuels are non-renewable because they take millions of years to form

explanations_tiered.definition: Non-renewable resources are defined by formation rate relative to consumption, not by environmental impact.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cleaner combustion does not alter the geological timescale of formation; natural gas remains non-renewable.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Just because natural gas makes less smoke than coal doesn't mean it grows back. It still takes millions of years to form.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Renewability is about replenishment rate, not emission profile. Natural gas forms from marine organisms over 50-100 million years.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The carbon in natural gas is fossil carbon, not part of the active biosphere carbon cycle; its use adds 'new' CO2 to the atmosphere and is not renewable.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of non-renewable: formation rate << consumption rate.
Natural gas forms over millions of years.
Cleanliness is irrelevant to renewability.
misconceptions: Students sometimes think 'cleaner' = 'renewable'. They must separate environmental impact from renewability.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Natural gas = non-renewable (formation time millions of years). Clean ≠ renewable.

glossary_entry.definition: Fossil fuel (methane) formed over millions of years, non-renewable.

flashcard.front: Is natural gas renewable because it burns cleaner than coal?
flashcard.back: No. Renewability depends on formation rate, not emissions.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is natural gas non-renewable despite being cleaner?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Formation takes millions of years; consumption rate exceeds formation rate.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that 'renewable' is about rate, not environmental impact.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Natural gas forms over millions of years, consumed faster than formed. Cleanliness is unrelated.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
questions.section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Green Legacy Initiative launched in Ethiopia in 2019?

options.A: To build new roads and highways
options.B: To plant billions of tree seedlings to combat environmental degradation

questions.hint: Green Legacy is about tree planting.
questions.explanation: The Green Legacy Initiative is a national tree-planting campaign aimed at building a green and climate-resilient Ethiopia. Option B describes this. Options A, C, D are not related.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Six: Population and natural resources
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
source_trace.content_anchor: Green Legacy initiative launched in June 2019 to build a green and climate resilient Ethiopia

explanations_tiered.definition: The Green Legacy Initiative is Ethiopia's national tree-planting program to restore degraded land.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Launched in 2019, it aims to plant billions of trees to combat deforestation, soil erosion, and climate change.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Green Legacy means planting many trees to make Ethiopia greener and stop land from washing away.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The initiative set a world record by planting over 350 million trees in one day (2019), focusing on degraded watersheds.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Afforestation under Green Legacy increases carbon sequestration, stabilises soils, and enhances water infiltration, reducing erosion.
reasoning_steps: Recall recent Ethiopian conservation initiative.
Identify that it involves massive tree planting.
Exclude unrelated infrastructure or fossil fuel options.
misconceptions: Some may confuse Green Legacy with other development projects; it is specifically tree planting.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Green Legacy = mass tree planting in Ethiopia (2019).

glossary_entry.definition: Ethiopian national tree-planting campaign for environmental restoration.

flashcard.front: What is Ethiopia's Green Legacy Initiative?
flashcard.back: A massive tree-planting campaign to fight land degradation and climate change.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Plant billions of trees to restore degraded land and build climate resilience.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Share the 2019 world record: 350 million trees in 12 hours.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 6.3 Conservation of natural resources in Ethiopia
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Green Legacy is a massive tree-planting campaign.
